claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm inquiring about. Their name is Alex, and they're 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 16 years old, I need to ask a few more questions to determine eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, attending school, college, or an approved training course)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex just left school recently, but they've signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. When Alex left education and registered with the careers service is important for determining eligibility.

**How many weeks ago did Alex leave school and register with the careers service?** (Child Benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education if they register with a government-sponsored careers service)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 10 weeks since Alex left school and registered with the careers service.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to understand your living situation and responsibility for Alex.

**Does Alex currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. And just to confirm a few more details:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. One final question:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, started an apprenticeship, or started receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't started any paid work, isn't doing an apprenticeship, and doesn't receive any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Even though Alex has left school, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since Alex registered with the local careers service and it's only been 10 weeks, you're still within this allowable period.

Additionally, all the other qualifying conditions are met:
- Alex lives with you in the UK
- Alex is under 20 years old
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex hasn't started working 24+ hours per week, an apprenticeship, or receiving their own benefits

**Important to note:** You have up to 20 weeks from when Alex left education and registered with the careers service. After that period, if Alex hasn't returned to approved education or training, your Child Benefit entitlement would end. So you'll want to keep track of when those 20 weeks are up (you have about 10 weeks remaining).

If you have any other questions, feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and left education 10 weeks ago but registered with a government-sponsored careers service. According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since only 10 weeks have passed (which is within the 20-week allowance period), Alex still qualifies. Additionally, Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex has not started paid work of 24+ hours per week, an apprenticeship, or started receiving benefits in their own right - all of which would disqualify them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (16 years old), who left education 10 weeks ago and registered with a careers service. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit. Alex hasn't started substantial paid work, an apprenticeship, or received benefits. Since Alex is within the 20-week grace period for 16-17 year olds registered with a careers service after leaving education, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit.
